text: The surgeon adjusts the patient's position to a reverse Trendelenburg to improve visualization of the gallbladder, which is obscured by excessive visceral fat.
The surgeon, using a robotic system, carefully dissects inflammatory adhesions from the bottom of the gallbladder, which is now better visualized with the patient positioned on a footboard.
The surgeon achieves improved retraction and visualization of the gallbladder, beginning dissection at the neck of the gallbladder.
The surgeon encounters a challenging dissection of the gallbladder, which appears edematous and thickened, complicating the procedure.

The surgeon attempts to maneuver the left robotic arm to reach higher on the gallbladder, encountering difficulty as the arm bumps against the right rib cage. The arm is positioned laterally and high, which may be causing the issue.
The surgeon performs further dissection around the gallbladder, exposing the cystic artery, which becomes visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon reveals the outline of the cystic duct and the lower portion of the gallbladder, highlighting their anatomical positions within the surgical field.
The surgeon accidentally nicks a venous branch or vessel on the posterior side of the cystic duct, resulting in visible bleeding within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the cystic duct to create enough space for clip placement, aiming to control bleeding from vessels located on the duct.
The surgeon prepares to take the cystic duct and artery, using multiple instruments to manage the structures during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a suction device to manage unexpected bleeding in the surgical field, possibly considering the use of an assist port for better visualization and control.
The surgeon carefully separates the gallbladder from the posterior aspect of the liver, while isolating a posterior branch of the cystic artery from the gallbladder tissue.
The surgeon uses a top-down approach to reach and detach the remaining attachments of the gallbladder from the liver, encountering difficulty with the left hand.
The final view shows the cystic artery and duct, with the common bile duct visible posteriorly in the surgical field.
The surgeon inspects the dissected areas for any signs of bleeding, ensuring hemostasis in the regions previously separated during the gallbladder removal.
